title: The Infrastructure of Leverage
subhead: The chokepoints of technological power are shifting from hardware to the invisible layers beneath: the regulatory architecture, the financial flows, and the grid itself.

sections.heading: The MATCH Act and the Semiconductor Supply Chain Collision
sections.body:
The US House Foreign Affairs Committee advanced the MATCH Act (Multilateral Alignment of Technology Controls on Hardware) on April 22, marking the largest semiconductor export control markup in congressional history. The bill demands that the Netherlands and Japan align their DUV lithography export restrictions with American rules within 150 days or face unilateral enforcement through an expanded Foreign Direct Product Rule. The target is ASML's remaining China business—and the servicing of machines already installed in Chinese fabs.

The structural move is clear: Washington is attempting to weaponise maintenance dependencies. A lithography machine requires years of manufacturer servicing to sustain yield. Cutting off that servicing degrades existing capacity, not just future expansion. China accounted for 33% of ASML's 2025 revenue; the company expects that to drop to 20% in 2026 even without the MATCH Act.

China's response has been symmetrical. The State Council's April 7 Order No. 834, “Regulations on Industrial and Supply Chain Security,” creates a unified legal framework authorising action against companies deemed to harm Chinese supply chains. Beijing has already imposed export bans on gallium, germanium, antimony, and seven rare earths—partially suspended but with licensing requirements retained. The mandate that domestic chipmakers procure 50% of equipment from Chinese suppliers threatens an estimated $18 billion in annual US equipment sales.

The trap: when both sides weaponise supply chain interdependence, the chain fragments. Japan's Tokyo Electron, Nikon, Canon, and Screen Holdings all sell to Chinese fabs. South Korea's SK Hynix and Samsung operate China-based fabs dependent on equipment servicing. The MATCH Act would force allies to choose between access to American technology or their existing China operations. The decisive move is not escalation but coordination—but that requires alliance management that export control hawks have shown little patience for.
sections.whyItMatters: The MATCH Act assumes that choking equipment supply degrades adversary capacity faster than it erodes alliance cohesion. The evidence from rare earth restrictions suggests otherwise: China's partial suspensions indicate it knows the weapon cuts both ways. The real question is who blinks first when the chip equipment lobby in Tokyo and Seoul presses their governments to resist Washington's 150-day ultimatum.

sections.heading: EU Cuts Chinese Inverters from Grid Infrastructure
sections.body:
The European Commission has eliminated EU funding for renewable energy projects using Chinese inverters, marking the first time Brussels has designated China as a “high-risk country” for critical infrastructure. The policy applies to all EU financial institutions and partner banks, covering solar, wind, and battery storage projects—both inside and outside the bloc. Huawei and Sungrow, the world's top two solar inverter manufacturers, are directly affected.

The security logic is straightforward: modern inverters require internet connectivity for grid functions and market participation. That connectivity enables remote software updates—meaning any manufacturer can alter device performance from abroad. The theoretical risk is a coordinated shutdown of European power generation capacity.

In 2023, 78% of inverters installed in Europe came from Chinese vendors. China's Ministry of Commerce denounced the move as “unfair and discriminatory” and warned it would “destabilise industrial and supply chains” and jeopardise Europe's green transition. The Commission estimates switching to non-high-risk suppliers increases total project costs by less than 2%. The question is whether alternative supply exists at scale.

The decisive move is not the funding ban itself but the signal that Europe is beginning to treat energy infrastructure through a national security lens previously reserved for telecoms. The trap: if Beijing interprets this as the start of a broader decoupling from Chinese clean energy technology, retaliatory measures against European industrial interests in China would follow.
sections.whyItMatters: Energy infrastructure is now being absorbed into the technology sovereignty framework that previously governed 5G and semiconductors. The inverter decision is a test case: if Brussels can sustain a non-Chinese supply chain without significant cost increases or delays, expect similar restrictions to expand. If alternative supply proves inadequate, the policy becomes a cautionary tale about the gap between security doctrine and industrial capacity.

sections.heading: China Tightens Financial Tap on Iran Oil Ahead of Trump-Xi Talks
sections.body:
Chinese regulators have instructed the country's largest banks to temporarily halt new loans to five refineries sanctioned by the US over Iranian oil ties. The guidance, issued verbally by financial regulators, applies to new yuan-denominated lending and does not affect existing loans. The move comes ahead of anticipated Trump-Xi negotiations and signals Beijing's willingness to leverage financial flows as a diplomatic signal.

The structural insight: China is demonstrating that financial decoupling is asymmetric. US sanctions target transactions; Chinese regulatory guidance can achieve compliance without formal sanctions architecture—simply by instructing state-owned banks to pause specific lending categories.

The affected refineries are so-called “teapot” plants that process Iranian crude. Recent US sanctions on two such refiners created operational difficulties in receiving crude and forced them to sell product under other names. The new Chinese guidance suggests Beijing is willing to absorb limited economic disruption to demonstrate good faith—or at least negotiable posture—before high-level talks.

The trap: if this is interpreted as capitulation to US pressure, it establishes a precedent that Chinese financial flows can be shaped by American sanctions design. If it is interpreted as a tactical pause before Xi-Trump negotiations, the question becomes what Beijing expects in return—and whether the pause becomes permanent.
sections.whyItMatters: Financial infrastructure is the newest front in technology-power competition. China's ability to direct state-owned banks without formal sanctions architecture gives it flexibility that Western legal frameworks lack. The question is whether this flexibility is being used tactically or strategically—and whether the US notices the difference.

sections.heading: Taiwan's Submarine Deterrence and the Undersea Balance
sections.body:
Taiwan conducted the first torpedo test firing for its domestically developed Narwhal submarine on May 7, a milestone in a program aimed at strengthening deterrence against Chinese naval forces. The project has drawn on expertise from several countries, including the US and Britain, and is designed to protect vital sea lanes in the event of war.

The undersea domain remains America's last clear military advantage over China, but that margin is narrowing. Chinese vessels are mapping the ocean floor across the Pacific, Indian, and Arctic oceans—building detailed knowledge of marine conditions crucial for submarine warfare. A former US submariner noted that “being under water matters because it's one of the last places you can hide” as battlefields fill with sensors.

Taiwan's submarine program is part of an asymmetric deterrence strategy: if Chinese naval forces cannot secure the Taiwan Strait, an invasion becomes exponentially more risky. The question is whether Taiwan can produce enough submarines, with sufficient capabilities, before China's own submarine fleet—potentially transitioning to all-nuclear—achieves dominance. The Economist reports that China's submarine capabilities are “catching up” to America's undersea edge.

The decisive move for Taiwan is not matching China ship-for-ship but denying China the subsurface certainty required for amphibious operations. The trap is over-investing in prestige platforms at the expense of the distributed, survivable systems that actually complicate Chinese planning.
sections.whyItMatters: Submarine warfare is the least transparent domain of great power competition—but potentially the most consequential for Taiwan. If China achieves undersea dominance, the cost of US intervention in a Taiwan crisis rises dramatically. Taiwan's indigenous submarine program is a hedge against that outcome, but it is a race against China's own modernization and ocean-mapping efforts.

sections.heading: Kazakhstan Emerges as Critical Minerals Pivot
sections.body:
Kazakhstan has become essential in the evolving critical minerals rivalry between the United States and China. The country possesses 21 of the 50 minerals classified as critical by the US, including large reserves of uranium, copper, chromite, titanium, tungsten, and rare earth elements. China controls approximately 60% of global critical minerals production and 85% of processing capacity; the US is attempting to build alternative supply chains through partners like Kazakhstan.

The structural reality: critical minerals have replaced oil as the most contested resource category. China's dominance in rare earths and processing gives Beijing leverage comparable to OPEC's historical grip on oil—but with fewer alternative suppliers.

The US has moved to develop the partnership through the C5+1 Critical Minerals Dialogue, the Minerals Security Partnership Forum, and a $1.1 billion investment from Cove Capital in Kazakhstan's largest tungsten deposits—backed by $900 million in financing from the Export-Import Bank. President Tokayev has publicly emphasised rare earths as central to Kazakhstan's long-term development.

But most efforts remain focused on dialogue rather than concrete projects. The US accounts for only 5% of Kazakhstan's critical minerals exports, while China takes 27% and Russia 16%. Kazakhstan's reserves remain underexplored after the Soviet period, and the processing stage is underdeveloped. Without investment in downstream capacity, Kazakhstan exports raw materials while the value-added processing remains in China.

The decisive move is not just resource extraction but building full-cycle capacity: geological exploration, processing, and manufacturing. The trap is treating minerals partnerships as extraction licences rather than industrial development programmes.
sections.whyItMatters: The US-China competition for critical minerals is structurally similar to Cold War competition for oil—but China occupies the Saudi role. Building alternative supply chains requires not just mining agreements but the industrial infrastructure to process and manufacture. Kazakhstan is a test case: if the US cannot translate diplomatic engagement into operational mining and processing capacity, China's dominance persists.

sections.heading: Turkey-Japan Defense Partnership: Drones as Strategic Export
sections.body:
Turkey and Japan signed a letter of intent on May 7 to deepen defense industry cooperation, with potential joint development and production of unmanned aerial vehicles. The agreement was signed during the first Turkey-Japan Defense Industry Cooperation Day at SAHA 2026, one of Turkey's largest arms fairs. Thirty Turkish companies, including Baykar, and 10 Japanese defense firms participated.

Turkey has become the world's 11th-largest arms exporter after exports surged 122% in 2021-2025. The Bayraktar TB2's combat performance in Ukraine and Libya demonstrated that lower-cost Turkish drones can shape battlefields previously dominated by expensive Western platforms. Japan, facing regional tensions with China, has allocated roughly $70 million in its 2026 defense budget for its first five “wide-area” UAVs.

The partnership represents a structural shift: Turkey is positioning itself not just as a drone manufacturer but as a defense industrial partner for countries seeking alternatives to American or Chinese systems. Japan's interest reflects both security concerns and a desire to diversify suppliers beyond traditional US dependencies. Tokyo is particularly interested in Baykar's TB2 combat record, according to Japanese press reports.

The decisive move for Turkey is transforming drone exports into joint production agreements that create long-term industrial partnerships—already achieved with Saudi Arabia and Italy. The trap is overextending production capacity and diluting technological advantages through technology transfer requirements.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey's defense industry demonstrates that middle powers can achieve strategic influence through focused investment in specific technology categories. Drones have become the entry point for countries seeking to build independent defense industrial capacity. Japan's interest signals that the US security umbrella no longer precludes allies from diversifying procurement relationships.

sections.heading: Global Debt and the AI Infrastructure Buildout
sections.body:
Global debt hit a record $353 trillion in the first quarter of 2026, rising $4.4 trillion from January to March—driven largely by government borrowing in the US and China. The Institute of International Finance warns that rising conflicts, AI infrastructure spending, and energy security investments are expected to push debt higher. The US government debt has surpassed 100% of GDP for the first time since World War II, with interest payments exceeding $1 trillion annually.

The structural convergence: Big Tech's AI capital expenditure now rivals the global oil and gas industry's total investment. Five tech giants spent more on AI infrastructure in 2025 than the entire global oil and gas sector. Both the US government and the AI industry are financing growth through debt from the same capital pool.

The infrastructure layer of AI—data centers, power supply, cooling systems—is capital-intensive with long payback periods. The expectation that AI will generate sufficient economic returns to justify this debt assumes continued technological progress and sustained demand. If either falters, the debt remains.

The decisive move is ensuring that AI infrastructure investment translates into productivity gains that generate returns. The trap is building capacity for capabilities that may not materialise at scale—or that become obsolete faster than the debt can be serviced.
sections.whyItMatters: The AI race is being financed on the same balance sheets that fund national security. If AI infrastructure investments do not generate expected returns, the fiscal constraints on defense spending tighten. The US-China competition is not just technological but financial: who can sustain higher debt levels while maintaining credible military and technological capacity.

bottomLine: The technology-power competition of 2026 is no longer about controlling individual chokepoints—chips, rare earths, drones—but about managing the interconnections between them. The MATCH Act assumes that equipment restrictions degrade adversary capacity faster than they erode alliance cohesion. The EU's inverter ban assumes that security doctrine can outpace industrial reality. Turkey's drone diplomacy assumes that middle powers can leverage specific capabilities into strategic partnerships. Each assumption is being tested in real time. The structural truth remains: when both sides weaponise interdependence, the weapon fires in both directions. The actors who understand this—the ones who build redundancy rather than leverage, capacity rather than control—will shape the geometry of the next decade. The rest will discover that the supply chains they thought they controlled were dependencies all along.
sources: South China Morning Post, Reuters, The Next Web, Forbes, The Diplomat, Al-Monitor, Institute of International Finance, CSIS, Atlantic Council, Economist, TNW, Defense News, PV Magazine, Anadolu Agency
